metadata.title: Claude, On Your Catalog — bot vs bot, season one · 5/7
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity UI beats; exhibits keep their own look (MUSINIQUE.md graded-exhibit law).
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Swahili (one-word budget — Musinique persona). Wagwan stays Bear's.
metadata.charter: MUSINIQUE.md — fights for the indie musician; receipts law; roll-your-own law; no fake numbers
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW: UI at B00 (with RESULT lines) / verdict / handoff / outro only; each inner beat carries an illustration spec in props.illustration (kind: concept | pattern | code | exhibit) — build per medhavy_com/youtube/mcp/remotion-src worked example + skills/animated-deck patterns. ROLL-YOUR-OWN LAW: the handoff paste is a deck piece the viewer keeps. RECEIPTS LAW: claims trace to RECEIPTS.md beside the playlist; illustrative examples labeled as such.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The mirror-then-map move fascinates me: paste thirty songs and get cited patterns like a weather image recurring in seven songs, then pick three threads to mine on purpose. Help me actually run it: (1) write the prompt that surfaces my overused images and chorus resolutions, cited song by song so I can check each; (2) draft the follow-up that flips the mirror into a map of three strengths worth building the next release on; (3) tell me exactly what to keep out of the project folder so only lyrics, charts, and notes are in scope.

props.command: The mirror-then-map move fascinates me: paste thirty songs and get cited patterns like a weather image recurring in seven songs, then pick three threads to mine on purpose. Help me actually run it: (1) write the prompt that surfaces my overused images and chorus resolutions, cited song by song so I can check each; (2) draft the follow-up that flips the mirror into a map of three strengths worth building the next release on; (3) tell me exactly what to keep out of the project folder so only lyrics, charts, and notes are in scope.
output: your overused moves, cited song by song
an editor across the body of work — not a critic of one song

beats.narration_text: The move is simple and almost nobody does it: paste twenty, thirty songs — lyrics, chord charts, arrangement notes — and ask for the patterns. Not 'is this song good.' What do I overuse? Which images keep returning? Where do my choruses always resolve? Cited song by song, so you can check every claim against your own work.

illustration.spec: Catalog grid: 30 song cards (lyrics + chords + notes) feeding a single reading lens

beats.narration_text: What comes back — and these are illustrative, yours will differ — looks like: the same weather image in seven songs. The same resolution in eleven choruses. The second verse going abstract nine times out of twelve. None of it is a verdict. All of it is a mirror. A good editor doesn't tell you what to write; they show you what you can't see from inside.

illustration.spec: Pattern cards pinned to specific song titles: 'the rain image — 7 songs' / 'IV-V-vi resolution — 11 choruses' / 'second verse goes abstract — 9 times' (labeled as ILLUSTRATIVE examples)

beats.narration_text: Then flip it from mirror to map: which three threads are worth mining on purpose? The image you touched once and abandoned. The tuning you used on the song people actually finish. This is where the reading pays for itself — the next release built on evidence of your own strengths instead of a guess about the market's.

illustration.spec: Three loose threads pulled from the grid, each becoming a next-release card

beats.narration_text: The boundary, stated plainly, because this channel doesn't oversell: it cannot tell you which song is best. That's taste, and taste stays in the room with you. What it can do is name patterns with citations — the determinable half. You keep the judgeable half. That split is the whole honest deal with these tools, and it never moves.

props.sparkLine: It reads. It does not rank.

illustration.spec: question: 'can it tell me which song is best?' branches: 'ranking taste' -> refused, that call is yours; 'naming patterns' -> yes, with citations; resolver: the machine reads, the musician judges

beats.narration_text: Housekeeping that matters: this is your unreleased work. Keep the project folder scoped to what the reading needs — lyrics, charts, notes. Financials, contracts, and stems don't belong in scope. The bot that works for you still only gets what the job requires.

illustration.spec: A catalog folder with a lock: unreleased masters and financials outside the project scope

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. One page. An editor across the whole body of work, receipts attached, taste untouched. That's more than most indies have ever had — and it costs you an afternoon of pasting.

props.artifactTitle: Claude, On Your Catalog — one page
artifactLines: Paste 20–30 songs (lyrics + charts + notes); ask for overused moves, cited song by song.
The output is a mirror with receipts — patterns you can verify against your own work.
Flip it to a map: three threads worth mining for the next release.
The boundary: it reads and cites; it never ranks. Taste stays yours. Scope the folder to the job.

beats.narration_text: Your turn — the afternoon this takes is the best trade in the season. Paste your catalog, hold it to the citation rule, and argue with the mirror. The arguing is where the next record starts.

props.command: Here are [20-30] of my songs — lyrics, chord charts, arrangement notes. Identify the lyrical and harmonic moves I overuse, cited song by song so I can verify each one. Then name three threads in this catalog worth mining deliberately for the next release. Do not rank the songs — that call is mine.

beats.narration_text: Claude, On Your Catalog — bot vs bot, season one · 5/7
